text: The surgeon dissects the mesogastrium at the left gastric epiploic vessel, dividing the middle section of the gastrocolic ligament with an ultrasonically activated device. The gastrocolic ligament is dissected at the left colic flexure using specialized gauze, with blunt separation of adipose tissues to expose the fascia plane. Mobilized adipose tissue appears smooth and intact. The dissection proceeds to the bottom of the left gastric epiploic vessel, where clipping and cutting occur. The greater omentum is cut off from left to right, revealing the trijunction point formed by the greater omentum, mesogastrium of the right gastroepiploic vessel, and mesocolon of the transverse colon. The trijunction point is identified, and dissection continues using gauze.

The surgeon cuts the adjacent part between the mesogastrium and the mesocolon of the transverse colon. The surgical field then shifts to the area around the head of the pancreas, where adipose tissue is meticulously cleaned away.

The surgeon exposes the superior mesenteric vein and removes surrounding adipose tissue to reveal the right gastroepiploic vein, which is clipped and cut. The right gastroepiploic artery is similarly clipped and cut. The gastroduodenal artery is dissected by cleaning small fibrous tissues between the superior border of the pancreas and the posterior wall of the gastric lesser curvature.

The model illustrates the anatomical structure of the mesogastrium related to the left gastric vessels. The surgeon incises the serosa along the superior border of the pancreas and turns attention to the common hepatic artery. The right part of the mesogastrium is cleaned up, exposing the root of the left gastric vein, which is clipped and cut. The thick sheet of the left gastric artery is dissected and exposed at its root, followed by triple clipping and cutting.

The surgeon mobilizes fibro tissues laterally, defining the adipose tissues on both sides as the mesogastrium of the left gastric artery. Dissection proceeds along the hepatic portal vein, with an incision made on the left side of the mesogastrium. The splenic artery is dissected until reaching the posterior gastric wall. The dissection continues at the lesser curvature, cleaning adipose tissues and nerves along the lesser curvature up to the upper part of the stomach. The right gastric vessels are exposed, clipped, and cut.
